**Investigations:** arterial blood gas (**indicated for signs or symptoms of hypoxia**), serial lactate, full blood count and platelets, clotting profile, blood culture, electrolytes urea and creatinine, blood glucose, liver function tests, and CRP and procalcitonin to monitor response.

**Management:** this is an emergency. Secure airway and give **oxygen to prevent hypoxia**. Since hypotension is refractory to fluid, **inotropic and vasopressor support is required** — management of hypotension and shock is a listed component of supportive therapy. **Correct acid/base and electrolyte imbalance**, and **prevent hypoglycaemia and hyperglycaemia**. **Maintain a thermoneutral environment.** **Correct anaemia**, and consider **exchange blood transfusion or buffy coat transfusion** where indicated — sclerema, bacterial hepatitis with high conjugated bilirubin, or leucopenia. Review and escalate antibiotics according to **local sensitivity patterns**, and repeat investigations, since sudden deterioration may occur. Note that **pentoxifylline**, which reduces TNF-α and improves microcirculation, has **shown better survival**.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Work up the ladder and place him

---

**SEPSIS** — infection with systemic signs ✓ he has this

**SEVERE SEPSIS** — sepsis + organ dysfunction or hypoperfusion ✓ he has this too

**SEPTIC SHOCK** — severe sepsis + **hypotension REFRACTORY to fluid resuscitation**

---

>> **"Two fluid boluses with no improvement in blood pressure"** — that phrase **is** the definition.

He is in **septic shock**.

---

## Step 2: Check every number against its threshold

---

| Finding | His value | Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| **Capillary refill** | **5 s** | > 3 s |
| **Systolic BP** | **38 mmHg** | < 65 mmHg (≤ 1 month) |
| **Lactate** | **5.2 mmol/L** | > 3 mmol/L |
| **Urine output** | **None for 8 hr** | Oliguria |

---

>> **Every single parameter is beyond its threshold.** This is not a borderline case.

---

## Step 3: Understand what the lactate is telling you

This is the most informative number here.

---

**Step 1:** Tissues are not receiving enough **oxygen**

**Step 2:** Cells switch to **anaerobic metabolism**

**Step 3:** Anaerobic metabolism produces **lactate**

**Step 4:** Lactate accumulates in blood

---

>> **A lactate of 5.2 means cells all over his body are currently starving of oxygen.**

It is not a marker of infection. It is a **direct measure of tissue oxygen debt**.

>> And it is one of the best things to follow: **a falling lactate means your treatment is working.**

---

## Step 4: Read the peripheries

**Mottled, cold peripherally, capillary refill 5 seconds.**

---

**What is happening?**

>> The body is **shutting down peripheral circulation** to preserve blood flow to the brain and heart.

Skin, gut and kidney are sacrificed first.

---

>> **Cold, mottled skin with slow refill is the body telling you it has already begun rationing.**

And capillary refill costs nothing to measure — no machine, no laboratory.

---

## Step 5: The oliguria is not just a fluid problem

**No urine for 8 hours.**

---

>> **Oliguria** is listed as a sign of **end organ damage** in severe sepsis.

The kidney is under-perfused, so filtration has stopped.

---

>> This tells you the shock is **not new**. Organs have been under-perfused long enough to fail.

And urine output remains one of the most useful ongoing monitors — when it returns, perfusion is returning.

---

## Step 6: Why did the fluid not work? Understand this properly.

This is the key physiological insight of the case.

---

**In hypovolaemic shock** (bleeding, dehydration):

- The container is normal size
- There is simply not enough in it
- **Fill it → pressure rises**

---

**In septic shock:**

**Step 1:** Inflammatory mediators cause **massive vasodilation** — the container gets **bigger**

**Step 2:** They cause **capillary leak** — the container **leaks**

**Step 3:** They cause **myocardial depression** — the pump is **weak**

---

>> **You cannot fill a container that is expanding and leaking, using a failing pump.**

That is exactly why the definition uses the word **refractory**.

---

## Step 7: So what does he actually need?

>> **Inotropes and vasopressors** — drugs that **constrict the vessels** and **support the heart**.

---

**They address the actual problems:**

- Vasopressors **shrink the container** back towards normal
- Inotropes **strengthen the failing pump**

---

>> **More fluid alone will not save him — and may worsen pulmonary oedema.**

Recognising the transition from "needs fluid" to "needs inotropes" is the whole point of the septic shock definition.

---

## Step 8: The supportive measures are not background noise

In a baby this sick, each one matters:

---

**Oxygen** — tissues are already in oxygen debt; the lactate proves it

**Correct acidosis** — acidosis **further depresses the myocardium** and reduces the response to inotropes, creating a vicious cycle

**Correct glucose** — sepsis disrupts glucose handling in both directions

**Thermoneutral environment** — a cold baby burns glucose and oxygen he cannot spare

**Correct anaemia** — oxygen is carried on haemoglobin; less haemoglobin means less delivery, whatever the perfusion

---

## Step 9: The two extra therapies worth knowing

---

**EXCHANGE OR BUFFY COAT TRANSFUSION**

>> Indications: **sclerema · bacterial hepatitis with high conjugated bilirubin · leucopenia**

It removes toxins and inflammatory mediators, and supplies **neutrophils, immunoglobulin, complement and clotting factors** — the very things the newborn is deficient in.

---

**PENTOXIFYLLINE**

>> It **reduces TNF-α** and **improves microcirculation**, and **showed better survival**.

---

>> Look at what it targets: **not the bacteria, but the body's damaging inflammatory response and the failing microcirculation.**

In a baby with a lactate of 5.2, improving microcirculation is aimed at exactly his problem.

Compare with granulocyte transfusions, GM-CSF and IVIG — all **of doubtful use**.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Refractory hypotension = septic shock. The word 'refractory' IS the diagnosis.**

>> **Lactate 5.2 = tissues starving of oxygen right now.**

>> **Fluid failed because the vessels are dilated and leaking and the heart is depressed — so he needs INOTROPES.**

>> **And keep him warm, oxygenated, sugared and non-acidotic while you do it.**
cards.tie:
This case completes the severity ladder.

And it teaches the most important transition in sepsis management:

**knowing the moment when more fluid stops being the answer.**

cards.q: KEY CUES — What words and findings should point you to neonatal sepsis and its stages?
cards.lecturer: Summary of the discriminating features drawn across the whole lecture.
cards.explain:
## The maternal history cues — MAJOR risk factors

**"Membranes ruptured more than 18 hours" / "PROM"** → **MAJOR**

**"Maternal fever > 38.0 °C in labour"** → **MAJOR**

**"Chorioamnionitis"** → **MAJOR**

**"Foul smelling / malodorous liquor"** → **MAJOR** — chorioamnionitis you can smell

**"Sustained fetal heart rate > 160"** → **MAJOR** — the fetus is responding

---

>> **One major risk factor alone justifies starting antibiotics — even in a well-looking baby.**

**ROM > 12 hr** and **fever 37.5–38.5 °C** are the **MINOR** versions of the same two.

---

## The timing cues

**"< 72 hours"** → **EARLY onset** → maternal genital tract, **GBS** (43–58%) in developed countries

**"4th–30th day"** → **LATE onset** → **nosocomial**, coagulase-negative *Staph*, *Candida*

**"In NICU beyond 30 days"** → **VERY LATE onset**

---

>> **In OUR setting:** the same organisms appear before and after 72 hours. Classify as **community** vs **hospital** acquired.

>> ***Klebsiella* heads BOTH of our lists.**

---

## The clinical cues

**"Not doing well"** → take it seriously. Often the **earliest** sign.

**"Poor feeding" / "was feeding, now won't"** → usually the **first** sign

**"Apnoea" in a neonate** → **sepsis until proven otherwise**

**"Temperature < 36 °C"** → hypothermia is **as dangerous as fever** — a SIRS criterion

**"Sclerema"** → **grave** sign; an indication for exchange/buffy coat transfusion

**"Petechiae and purpura"** → thrombocytopenia or **DIC**

**"Unexplained hypo- or hyperglycaemia"** → sepsis

**"Full fontanelle" / "high-pitched cry"** → think **meningitis** — but it may present with **none** of these

---

>> **Remember: meningitis can present as respiratory distress or diarrhoea.** Signs do not localise.

---

## The WHO SEVEN — any one means refer immediately

1. **Difficulty feeding**
2. **Convulsions**
3. **Movement only when stimulated**
4. **Respiratory rate > 60/min**
5. **Severe chest indrawing**
6. **Axillary temperature > 37.5 °C or < 35.5 °C**
7. **Cyanosis and grunting**

---

## The numbers — learn these cold

| Parameter | Threshold |
|---|---|
| Heart rate | **≥ 180 or ≤ 100** |
| Respiratory rate | **> 40** with grunting/desaturation |
| Glucose intolerance | **> 10 mmol/L** |
| Systolic BP day 1 | **< 50 mmHg** |
| Systolic BP ≤ 1 month | **< 65 mmHg** |
| **Capillary refill** | **> 3 seconds** |
| **Lactate** | **> 3 mmol/L** |
| WBC | **< 5,000 or > 34,000/mm³** |
| Absolute neutrophil | **< 1,000/mm³** |
| **Band : total ratio** | **> 0.2** |
| Immature neutrophils | **> 10%** |
| **Platelets** | **< 100,000** |
| **CRP** | **> 10 mg/L** |
| Procalcitonin | **> 2–2.5 ng/mL** after 72 hr |
| IL-6 / IL-8 | **> 70 pg/mL** |
| **Töllner score** | **<5 normal · 5–10 suspected · >10 definite** |

---

## The staging cues

**"Organ dysfunction" · "ARDS" · "≥2 organs failing"** → **SEVERE SEPSIS**

**End organ damage:** low PaO₂/FiO₂ · **no bowel sounds (ileus)** · **oliguria** · raised bilirubin · **thrombocytopenia**

**Hypoperfusion:** **raised lactate** · low BP · **slow capillary refill** · altered mental status

---

**"Hypotension REFRACTORY to fluid resuscitation"** → **SEPTIC SHOCK** → **needs INOTROPES, not more fluid**

---

## The investigation traps

>> **"Blood culture negative"** → **means almost nothing.** Positive in only **5–10%** of neonatal sepsis.

>> **"Superficial swab grew an organism"** → **poor correlation. NOT recommended.** These sites are colonised, not sterile.

>> **"Tracheal aspirate in a long-intubated baby"** → **NOT recommended** — rapid colonization.

>> **"Urine culture in EARLY onset"** → **NOT useful.** It is indicated in **LATE** onset only.

>> **"No neurological signs, so no LP"** → **WRONG.** Primary diagnosis of sepsis is itself an indication.

---

## The category cues

**Risk factors only, no symptoms needed** → **SUSPECTED**

**Signs + 1 marker (CRP or IL-6/8), culture negative** → **POSSIBLE**

**Signs + ≥2 abnormal labs, culture negative** → **PROBABLE**

**Full clinical + lab picture, no organism** → **CLINICAL**

**Organism from a STERILE site** → **PROVEN**

>> **Coagulase-negative needs TWO positive cultures**, or one plus a positive CRP — because it is a skin contaminant.

---

## Treatment cues

**"Which antibiotic?"** → **LOCAL sensitivity pattern.** Not the textbook.

**"Klebsiella"** → resistant to gentamicin **60–72%**, 3rd gen cephalosporins **57–66%**

**"Nosocomial"** → **vancomycin or fluoroquinolone + amikacin**

**"Aminoglycoside given"** → **monitor renal function**

**"Sclerema / leucopenia / bacterial hepatitis with high conjugated bilirubin"** → **exchange or buffy coat transfusion**

**"Reduces TNF-α, improves microcirculation, better survival"** → **PENTOXIFYLLINE**

**"Granulocyte transfusion / GM-CSF / IVIG"** → **doubtful use**

---

## Prevention cues

**"GBS positive mother"** → **intrapartum penicillin 2–4 hours before delivery**

**"Cord care"** → **4% chlorhexidine gel, once daily, 5–7 days**

**"Eye care"** → **0.5% erythromycin eye ointment, stat**

**"First hour after birth"** → **skin-to-skin on mother's abdomen**

And the one principle behind all of it:

>> **There is no definitive test. Treat on suspicion — then review, and stop when sepsis is ruled out.**
